disease.name: Swallowed Maternal Blood
disease.note: Benign cause of neonatal hematemesis/melena in first 24-48h of life. Baby swallows maternal blood during delivery (via birth canal, perineal tear, episiotomy) or from cracked/bleeding nipples during breastfeeding. KEY: Apt test (alkali denaturation) differentiates fetal Hb (pink, resistant) from adult Hb (brown/yellow denatured). ESSENTIAL to differentiate from VKDB and other coagulopathies.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Benign condition - no treatment needed; confirm with Apt test

subtypes.name: Ingested During Delivery
subtypes.notes: Swallowed maternal blood from birth canal during vaginal delivery

subtypes.name: Ingested from Breastfeeding
subtypes.notes: Cracked or bleeding nipples, blood mixed with breast milk

secondLine.test: Blood type and Rh (if mother and baby mismatch)

investigations.goldStandard: Apt test positive for adult hemoglobin + normal coagulation studies

outpatient: Reassurance for parents - benign self-limited condition
No intervention needed - resolves spontaneously in 24-48h
If breastfeeding: check for cracked nipples and treat (lanolin, positioning)
Educate parents about signs of true GI bleeding (lethargy, pallor, poor feeding)
preventive: Breastfeeding education: proper latch to prevent cracked nipples

disease.pathophysiology: During vaginal delivery, newborn swallows maternal blood from perineal lacerations, episiotomy, placental bleeding, or ruptured umbilical cord. During breastfeeding, cracked/bleeding nipples allow maternal blood to mix with breast milk. Ingested maternal blood passes through infant GI tract → appears as hematemesis (if recently swallowed and vomited) or melena (if partially digested). Gastric acid denatures hemoglobin → bright red becomes coffee-ground. The Apt test distinguishes fetal HbF (alkali-resistant) from adult HbA (alkali-denatures to brown) — the key diagnostic test.

complications.complication: Unnecessary workup for coagulopathy if not recognized

mustNotMiss: BEFORE diagnosing swallowed maternal blood, ALWAYS rule out VKDB - check PT/PTT and vitamin K prophylaxis history
Apt test is essential - do NOT skip it
VKDB is a PREVENTABLE life-threatening emergency - NEVER assume swallowed blood without confirming normal coagulation
Swallowed blood presents in first 24-48h - if bleeding starts after day 2, think VKDB

diagnosticClues.clue: Healthy, well-appearing newborn with hematemesis/melena in first 24-48h of life
Apt test positive for adult hemoglobin (maternal blood)
Vitamin K prophylaxis was given at birth

exclusionClues.clue: Sick appearing infant with abdominal distension (NEC)
Bleeding from other sites (umbilicus, circumcision) - suggests coagulopathy
Onset after day 2 of life (unlikely swallowed blood)
